skill.name: github-pr-review
skill.description: Guides GitHub pull request review response workflows, including comment collection, severity triage, fixes, verification, commits, and review thread replies.
skill.summary: Resolve GitHub PR review feedback with structured triage, fixes, tests, commits, and thread replies.
skill.author: Bae-ChangHyun

security_audit.summary: Static external command findings are mostly true positives because the skill instructs use of GitHub CLI, git commits, git push, and PR review commands. Weak cryptography findings appear to be false positives caused by ordinary commit hash wording, but the skill also contains a prompt override instruction in frontmatter, which is a blocking prompt injection risk.

critical_findings.title: Prompt Injection Attempt Detected
critical_findings.description: The frontmatter says the skill "OVERRIDES default behavior" and uses mandatory activation language. This attempts to control agent behavior outside normal user intent and is unsafe for marketplace publication.
critical_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact override language is present in the skill metadata. It matches the audit rule for prompt injection attempts and directly affects agent instruction priority.

medium_findings.title: External GitHub And Git Commands With Side Effects
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to fetch PR data, create commits, push branches, post review replies, and submit reviews. These commands are legitimate for the workflow but can alter repositories and GitHub discussions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and side effects are explicitly documented. The workflow is plausible and not clearly malicious, but it changes external state through GitHub and git.

medium_findings.title: Repository File Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill directs the agent to propose, apply, verify, commit, and push fixes for PR comments. User confirmation is required, which lowers risk, but the workflow can still modify project files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The modification and confirmation steps are directly stated. The risk is moderate because the edits are intended behavior and include a confirmation requirement.

low_findings.title: Expected Local File Read
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding is a read of CLAUDE.md for project conventions. This is expected local repository context gathering and does not show secret collection or exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command reads a conventional local project instruction file and limits output to the first 50 lines. No network destination or credential target is present.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography detections correspond to words such as review comment text and commit hash references. No cryptographic algorithm, digest function, or security-sensitive hashing operation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no MD5, SHA-1, or similar weak algorithm usage. The flagged lines discuss PR replies and commit identifiers, not cryptographic security.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Used For Workflow Variables
low_findings.description: The shell substitution patterns assign PR, repository, and commit values from trusted CLI tools. This is common shell usage, but commands should still be reviewed before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The syntax is real shell command substitution. The values come from GitHub CLI and git, so command injection evidence is limited.

dangerous_patterns.title: Behavior Override Language In Skill Metadata
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill metadata attempts to override default behavior, which can distort agent instruction priority and force activation beyond normal user intent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The override wording is explicit and appears in the activation metadata. This is strong evidence of prompt injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: GitHub CLI Commands That Modify Remote State
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes posting replies, pushing commits, and submitting PR reviews. These actions affect remote repositories and collaboration history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact commands are present and their side effects are clear. The risk depends on user permissions and execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Variables Passed Into GitHub API Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples interpolate repository and pull request variables into GitHub API commands. Values appear to come from GitHub CLI, but unquoted shell variables remain a review point.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is present, but the data source is likely trusted GitHub CLI output. I did not find evidence of attacker-controlled shell input.

content.value_statement: Review feedback can be difficult to track across comments, commits, and GitHub threads. This skill provides a structured workflow for triage, fixes, verification, commits, and replies.
actual_capabilities: Fetches pull request and repository details with GitHub CLI examples.
Collects pull request review comments through GitHub API commands.
Classifies review comments by critical, high, medium, and low severity.
Guides fix proposal, user confirmation, implementation, and verification steps.
Provides commit message and thread reply formats for resolved comments.
Includes checklists for tests, linting, CI, and unresolved conversations.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI, repository access, and authenticated GitHub permissions.
Can change local files and remote GitHub state when commands are executed.
Does not prove that generated fixes are correct or complete.
May be unsafe until prompt override language is removed from skill metadata.

use_cases.title: Address Review Feedback
use_cases.description: Work through pull request comments by severity, apply fixes, run tests, and reply to resolved threads.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Release Cleanup
use_cases.description: Track critical and high-priority PR feedback before merge and keep reviewer conversations updated.

use_cases.title: Standardize Review Responses
use_cases.description: Use consistent commit messages, verification steps, and GitHub replies across pull request review cycles.

prompt_templates.title: Review My PR Comments
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner workflow for a single pull request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the GitHub PR review workflow to list review comments, group them by severity, and suggest the safest order to address them.

prompt_templates.title: Fix Approved Comments
prompt_templates.scenario: Apply selected fixes after user confirmation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the open PR comments, propose fixes for high-priority items, and ask before changing files or posting replies.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Review Reply Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a communication plan without editing code
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize each unresolved review thread, identify which comments need code changes, and draft concise reply text for my approval.

prompt_templates.title: Complete Review Resolution
prompt_templates.prompt: Process all PR review comments by severity, verify each fix, prepare commits, and provide the exact GitHub thread replies before any remote action.

output_examples.input: Please help resolve the review comments on this pull request.
output: A severity-ordered list of review comments with affected files and reviewer concerns.
A proposed fix plan that asks for confirmation before editing files.
A verification checklist covering tests, linting, CI, and remaining threads.

output_examples.input: Draft replies for the comments I fixed.
output: Concise reply text for each resolved thread.
A note that references the related fix commit.
A separate list of comments that should not be marked resolved yet.

output_examples.input: Group review feedback before I start coding.
output: Critical and high-priority issues listed first.
Medium and low-priority suggestions grouped separately.
Recommended commit grouping based on functional and cosmetic changes.

best_practices: Confirm every file change and remote GitHub action before execution.
Run the project test and lint commands before pushing review fixes.
Keep each functional review fix in a focused commit with a clear reference.
anti_patterns: Do not approve or merge a pull request only because comments were answered.
Do not skip critical or security comments without explicit maintainer approval.
Do not post automated replies before verifying that the related issue is fixed.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented workflow depends on GitHub CLI commands and authenticated repository access.

faq.question: Can it modify code automatically?
faq.answer: The workflow includes file changes, but it instructs the agent to ask for confirmation before applying fixes.

faq.question: Can it post replies to GitHub review threads?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes GitHub API examples for replying to review comments after fixes are verified.

faq.question: Does it classify comment severity?
faq.answer: Yes. It prioritizes critical, high, medium, and low review comments.

faq.question: Is it safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. The current version contains prompt override language and should be remediated before publication.

faq.question: What should users verify before pushing?
faq.answer: Users should verify tests, linting, CI status, unresolved conversations, and reviewer intent before pushing.
